draft.introduction: The great penitential psalm, attributed to David after his sin with Bathsheba. Its theology of inward transformation—“create in me a clean heart”—shaped both Jewish and Christian understanding of repentance.

translation.text: Have mercy on me, O God, according to your steadfast love; according to your abundant mercy blot out my transgressions. Wash me thoroughly from my iniquity, and cleanse me from my sin. Against you, you only, have I sinned.
translation.original_text: ἐλέησόν με ὁ θεὸς κατὰ τὸ μέγα ἔλεός σου

translation.text: Create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me. Cast me not away from your presence, and take not your Holy Spirit from me. The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise.
translation.original_text: καρδίαν καθαρὰν κτίσον ἐν ἐμοί ὁ θεός

translator_notes.note: The verb κτίσον (“create”) uses the same root as κτίσις (“creation”). The psalmist asks not for repair but for an entirely new creation within—a move the Fathers connected to baptismal regeneration.
